title_en: Introduction and the Science of Fiqh
content_en:
Al-Mulakhas al-Fiqhi opens by establishing the foundations upon which Islamic jurisprudence rests. Sheikh Salih al-Fawzan begins with a definition of fiqh itself — the knowledge of the practical rulings of the Shari'ah derived from their detailed proofs. He distinguishes fiqh from related sciences: aqeedah deals with beliefs, while fiqh addresses outward actions. The author explains that Islamic jurisprudence is not a human invention but a God-given framework legislated for the benefit of humanity in this world and the next.

The chapter outlines the four primary sources of Islamic law: the Quran, the Sunnah, ijma' (scholarly consensus), and qiyas (analogical reasoning). Sheikh al-Fawzan discusses the hierarchy of these sources and explains that the Quran and Sunnah are the primary roots, while ijma' and qiyas serve as derivative sources that must ultimately trace back to textual evidence. He emphasizes that no human opinion, regardless of its source, can override an explicit text from the Book of Allah or the authentic Sunnah of His Messenger.

The author then introduces the concept of ijtihad — the exertion of scholarly effort to derive rulings from the sources — and explains the conditions that must be met before a scholar may engage in independent legal reasoning. He warns against unqualified persons issuing fatwas and stresses the communal obligation (fard kifayah) of preserving and transmitting Islamic legal knowledge across generations.

Sheikh al-Fawzan addresses the historical development of fiqh from the era of the Companions through the formation of the recognized madhabs. He explains that the four schools — Hanafi, Maliki, Shafi'i, and Hanbali — represent refined efforts of scholarly synthesis and that following one of them is permissible and praiseworthy, provided one understands that the ultimate authority remains with evidence. The chapter closes by situating this text within the Hanbali tradition while noting positions from other schools throughout, equipping the reader to understand the broad structure of Islamic jurisprudential discourse.
